main_summary.summary: Peggy Jean Connor, born in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, gives a November 30, 2015 interview with Emilye Crosby at the McCain Library and Archives at the University of Southern Mississippi for the Civil Rights History Project. Connor describes a ten-year effort to register to vote that culminated in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day. She collected poll taxes for beauty-shop customers, attended a non-violence workshop at the Dorchester Center with Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young, and taught citizenship classes at True Light Church in Hattiesburg. After SNCC's Guyot brought her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer spoke, Connor became a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party organizer and was elected as a delegate to the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City. She served as MFDP executive secretary and was the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the redistricting suit that produced Black-majority districts in Jackson. Connor recounts her arrest at a Hattiesburg courthouse march, hosting Rep. John Conyers Jr. at her home during Freedom Day, her daughter Felicia integrating Jefferson Davis Elementary School under freedom-of-choice, the Vernon Dahmer Sr. firebombing aftermath, and the lawsuit she filed against a Hattiesburg drugstore owner who refused to serve her in July 1964.
key_themes: Hattiesburg COFO office and citizenship classes
main_summary.historical_significance:
Connor is the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the Mississippi redistricting suit that produced majority-Black legislative districts in Jackson, and she served as executive secretary of the MFDP, making her testimony a primary source for the institutional mechanics of the party (record-keeping, board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson, candidate-litigation strategy with New York attorneys). Her account of the 1964 Atlantic City credentials challenge documents the gender split she observed inside the MFDP delegation over the two-seat compromise, men inclined to accept, women including Fannie Lou Hamer refusing.

Her ground-level recollections add Hattiesburg-specific texture to multiple canonical Movement events: the COFO building burning (after the Movement), the Dahmer firebombing on January 10, 1966 (her recollection of visiting Dahmer at the hospital before his death, and her belief that the NAACP had previously expelled Dahmer for working with SNCC), the Freedom Day march, hosting Rep. John Conyers Jr. during Freedom Day, and her daughter Felicia's freedom-of-choice integration of Jefferson Davis Elementary School. The interview also documents Hattiesburg's local KKK-customer-clientele intelligence network from a Black beautician's vantage point.

chapters.title: You Go Reddish to Vote
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the McCain Library at the University of Southern Mississippi on November 30, 2015, Peggy Jean Connor describes being born in Hattiesburg, a shy student who made good grades, and her teacher Ruby M. Tatum, who taught seven grades and told her class that when they turned 21 they should go to the circuit clerk's office and register to vote.

chapters.title: I Collected Poll Tax in My Beauty Shop
chapters.summary: Connor recounts trying to register at 21 and taking about ten years to succeed, paying the two-dollar poll tax for two consecutive years, and collecting poll tax money from her beauty-shop patrons who were afraid to go themselves, walking lists of names into the office and bringing back receipts for everyone.

chapters.title: Washing Windows All Downtown
chapters.summary: She describes her father working at a lumber yard and later driving an owner around and washing windows for nearly every business downtown, her mother as a housewife, and her aunt giving her the beauty shop when she left Hattiesburg, which is how Connor came to own it.

chapters.title: A Family of Beauticians
chapters.summary: Connor tells how she began at her aunt's shop at age 11 earning ten cents a shampoo, graduated from her aunt's beauty school in 1946, and describes a wide family of beauticians, her aunt married to a carpenter-minister, and an uncle who ran Franklin Beauty School in Texas.

chapters.title: He Made the Girls Mow Yard Too
chapters.summary: She recalls being paid the full price like her aunt, her strict father who made his three daughters mow the yard and his one son do housework, the sisters spoiling their baby brother, her mother working in white families' homes and being paid in cast-off goods, and that citizenship was taught in her Black school.

chapters.title: Twelve Days Before Freedom Day
chapters.summary: Connor describes how long it took her to register, asking her retired teacher who admitted she herself had never registered, and finally getting registered in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day, after a court order opened the rolls, though she received no voter card.

chapters.title: Eighteen of Us Walked In
chapters.summary: She recounts taking 18 people to the white Democratic precinct meeting at the library on Main Street in 1964, where the regulars, who voted Republican but met as Democrats, did not know what to do and telephoned the registrar Theron Lynd for instructions.

chapters.title: The Only One Who Could Participate
chapters.topic: A Rule Made on the Spot
chapters.summary: Connor explains that Theron Lynd ruled by phone that only she, the single registered voter among the 18, could participate, that she was voted to go on to the county meeting in 1964, helped count paper ballots at the polls, and was later voted to the district level in 1968.

chapters.title: Secretary of Both Organizations
chapters.summary: She describes her father as secretary of both the NAACP and another political group tied to the post-Brown era, having her and her sister Joyce write meeting letters as children, his job as a porter at the Hattiesburg American, and his anger at learning some NAACP members were reporting to whites.

chapters.title: A Different Person When He Came Back
chapters.summary: Connor tells of marrying a schoolmate who returned from the Korean War an alcoholic who loved to fight, their son and daughter born while he was stationed in Greenville, his drunken outburst at her father's house, and his mother coming from Gulfport to take him away for good.

chapters.title: She Was in an Iron Lung
chapters.summary: She recounts her second daughter contracting polio at age two during a local epidemic, the pediatrician Dr. Temple arranging emergency trips by car to iron lungs in Big Spring, Texas and later closer to home, the child's aneurysm and seizures, and her sharp wit watching television from her sickbed.

chapters.title: He Cried Like a Baby
chapters.summary: Connor describes the girl's father coming to the funeral and weeping, confessing a guilty act, her children all calling her own father Daddy after she moved back to her parents' home, and her recollection that the daughter, very close to her brother, died around 1953.

chapters.title: The COFO Office Across the Street
chapters.summary: She tells how the COFO office sat across Mobile Street from her beauty shop in a building a woman donated rent-free, how she watched the commotion from her doorway not being the type to walk over and ask, and recalls that the brick building later burned and Dr. Tussle saved some bricks for the archive.

chapters.title: That Woman Set Me on Fire
chapters.summary: Connor recalls a SNCC worker coming across to invite her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer was the speaker who set her on fire, after which Victoria Gray Adams invited her to a week-long nonviolence workshop taught by Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young.

chapters.title: Bring It Back to True Light
chapters.summary: She describes the Dorchester Center workshop on the South Carolina-Georgia line, returning home charged to start citizenship classes, asking the pastor of True Light church who let her hold them after Wednesday prayer meeting, and that her students went to register though none could do it right away because of the court order.

chapters.title: He Really Listened
chapters.summary: Connor praises the leader she calls G.I., describing him as a good listener who could explain people's reasoning back to them and did not back down, and recalls him speaking against the Vietnam War on the courthouse steps to community disapproval and the discovery that an informant was reporting on the meetings.

chapters.title: I Joined the NAACP for Insurance
chapters.summary: She explains joining the NAACP for the legal help it offered, worrying over her son and brother, and recounts her son Johnny being harassed and arrested by police at her father's shoeshine parlor, the two officers contradicting each other in court, and the judge advising her father to send the boy away, after which Johnny left Mississippi for Louisiana.

chapters.title: The Humblest He Could Be
chapters.summary: Connor remembers Clyde Kennard, who tried to enter Southern, as a humble and determined man she saw getting coffee at Fats Kitchen on Mobile Street, recalls not following his trial closely, and describes Medgar Evers coming through in the 1950s to organize and a starched, proud woman singing Go Tell It on the Mountain at a mass meeting.

chapters.title: She Could Wear Those Shorts
chapters.summary: Placing the mass meeting after the March on Washington in late 1963, Connor recalls the organizer Jean Wheeler, who stayed at the school and helped organize the MFDP before moving away, and who spent the summer of 1964 in Philadelphia, Mississippi to face down the Klan.

chapters.title: You'll Get Burned Up in That Shop
chapters.summary: She describes managing her shop alone while attending movement events, rival beauticians frightening her clients with warnings they would be burned up in her shop, the customer whose Klansman husband talked openly in front of her, and her work at the precincts during the Meredith crisis at Oxford.
related_events: Integration of the University of Mississippi

chapters.title: They Don't Count Us
chapters.summary: Connor recounts working the polls and counting votes for years without being bothered, often for little or no pay, telling another worker that white officials talked freely in front of them because they did not count Black people as a threat, and her uncertainty about how the election commission worked back then.

chapters.title: I Was Getting My Freedom
chapters.summary: She describes the Freedom Day march looping all the way around the courthouse past the Masonic Temple and furniture store, the menace of out-of-state lawmen brought in, the worker who arrived the night before whom she liked despite his reputation, and telling a writer she was out there getting her freedom.

chapters.title: A Shotgun Across His Leg
chapters.topic: Ministers and Guards at Her House
chapters.summary: Connor recalls the pouring rain on Freedom Day, a downtown store giving the police rain coats, John Conyers Jr. staying at her house with FBI agents to guard the congressman, and her father, recovering from paralysis, sitting up at night with a cane that people rumored was a shotgun across his leg.

chapters.title: My First Time on an Airplane
chapters.summary: She tells of returning from the Atlantic City convention in New Jersey, flying for the first time to New York to campaign for the MFDP ticket while another delegate went to Philadelphia, and a sudden demand that her overdue telephone bill be paid before she could travel.

chapters.part: Schools, Jail, and the Freedom Party
chapters.title: What School You Want to Send That Girl To
chapters.summary: Connor describes her daughter Felicia being one of six to desegregate the white schools under freedom of choice, returning from California to register her in time, learning the school was named Jefferson Davis Elementary, and how white children were seated before Felicia was walked in so there would be no incident.

chapters.part: Schools, Jail, and the Freedom Party
chapters.title: March On Back to That House
chapters.summary: She recounts being arrested while picketing the courthouse, an officer telling the marchers they looked good enough to march back to the jail, being moved from the jail to a work farm to keep leaders from the others, and a jail crowd of about a hundred singing songs and telling stories with a single dipper of water on the floor.

chapters.part: Schools, Jail, and the Freedom Party
chapters.title: Nine O'Clock We Were Ready
chapters.topic: A Week in Jail and Bond
chapters.summary: Connor describes not worrying about her children in jail, her mother and children never attending mass meetings while she and her father did, walking from the COFO office up Mobile Street to the courthouse knowing they would be jailed, and a couple posting their thousand-dollar house as bond that everyone got back.

chapters.part: Schools, Jail, and the Freedom Party
chapters.title: Mine Was the Only Vote
chapters.summary: She explains how, after the Dorchester workshop, SNCC organizers had them attend the white primary meetings, how the 18 had only her single registered vote, and how she alone was elected to the county meeting while getting no vote at the heated district meeting, trusting the Lord to take care of her.

chapters.title: I Bought a Box of Dixie Paper
chapters.summary: Connor tells of integrating a downtown drugstore in July 1964 after a longtime customer urged her, bringing her pregnant sister-in-law, sitting on separate stools while the men scattered with their plates, the owner saying he was sorry but could not serve them, and her filing suit.

chapters.title: You Can Sit Between My Legs
chapters.summary: She recounts the four-year course of her suit and a letter the owner sent her, learning the man seated beside her at the drugstore was the manager of Butler's shoe store who had fitted her wide feet, and confronting him that he could kneel between her legs to sell shoes but would not share a lunch counter, then walking out.

chapters.title: They Moved Me Out of There
chapters.summary: Connor reflects that she had always been shy and would never have let her son sit in, recalls her daughter wanting a treat at a counter, and tells how giving her first oral history on September 11 changed her, talking two hours, and how she and that interviewer mark each anniversary by calling each other.

chapters.title: Better Than Three Thousand
chapters.summary: She describes canvassing door to door, some people uninterested and others won over once the students arrived, and how registering for the Freedom Democratic Party, which did not require a courthouse visit, brought in better than three thousand people.

chapters.title: The Women That Saved Us
chapters.summary: Connor recalls riding three buses to Atlantic City with all their papers, believing they would be seated until Lyndon Johnson blocked it, and how the men of the group, listening to Adam Clayton Powell Jr. and others, wanted the two-seat compromise while the women, led by Fannie Lou Hamer, refused it.

chapters.title: Just Me and Her in Her Car
chapters.summary: She describes traveling the state with Victoria Gray Adams, going to a late-night WDAM interview, never being asked to join the Jackson organizing, and how for years the delegation photographs and history left her out because she was quiet, until Adams's room produced the pictures.

chapters.title: She Don't Do That Anymore
chapters.summary: Connor tells how, returning to the COFO office, a worker said papers were ready for her and an unseen voice said she did not do that anymore, because Annie Devine had been made secretary of the party, and how she walked out that day and never went back, unwilling to do anything to hurt the movement.

chapters.title: It Was So Pretty Down There
chapters.summary: She recounts going to Washington with the group for the congressional challenge, some shunning her, Annie Devine, Mrs. Hamer, and Mrs. Adams getting on the floor, and the delegation of people lining the hallway between the House buildings as the congressmen walked through.

chapters.title: He Thinks It Worked
chapters.summary: Connor recalls Michael Thelwell of SNCC, mostly in Washington, telling her the hallway line had moved some votes, attending statewide COFO and MFDP meetings, and being the first to sign a petition at a heated meeting and getting nominated to head the party though the people did not know her, naming others from Hattiesburg who went.

chapters.title: Some Strong Women
chapters.summary: She names SNCC people who stood out, Prathia Hall and Ella Baker, who spoke at Hattiesburg mass meetings, recalls her long talks with Fannie Lou Hamer and learning belatedly of Hamer's death around 1977 and of Victoria Gray Adams's death, and that Adams was funeralized at a Hattiesburg church.

chapters.title: They Put Him Out to Get You
chapters.summary: Connor describes knowing the movement supporter she calls Mr. Damon, hearing the NAACP had put him out for working with SNCC and housing SNCC people, seeing the ministers' letter, and learning the local chapter would not help when his house was firebombed because he had sheltered the movement.

chapters.title: Still Smoking That Morning
chapters.summary: She recalls going out early to his house still smoking, visiting Mr. Damon dying in the hospital where he was talking when she left, her disbelief that he died of smoke from the fire, and the shotgun and .38 her family kept locked up at home on busy Mobile Street.

chapters.title: Somebody in the White Community
chapters.summary: Connor tells of a carpenter warning her a man was asking where she lived, a William Carey College professor who helped rebuild the burned house, and her sense that some white people quietly aided the movement from the start, and explains she skipped the Freedom Schools to earn money in her shop.

chapters.title: The First One in the United States
chapters.summary: She recounts the Child Development Group of Mississippi, the delegates meeting Sargent Shriver in Washington who told them theirs would be the first Head Start in the United States, the program being taken away after Mississippi first refused it, and her own community-worker job around 1965.

chapters.title: You Couldn't Do Political Work
chapters.summary: Connor describes taking a Head Start community-worker job because she needed money even though it barred political work, suspecting that rule cost her the position, and explaining that her name was on the Connor v. Johnson suit because she was the party's executive secretary and record keeper the government would pursue.

chapters.title: He Took Me Down to the Bank
chapters.summary: She compares Guyot to a drill sergeant insisting on good financial records, recalls him taking her to open her first checking account though she had run a cash business for years, his staying less than a year, and his later life after being badly beaten in New York, helped by the worker Sheila.

chapters.title: The Lawyers in Khaki Pants
chapters.topic: Board Meetings and the New York Lawyers
chapters.summary: Connor recalls the worker Sheila who ran the office, housing movement people including a small woman named Mary, the open-door spirit where no one thought themselves better, MFDP board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson, and the New York lawyers in khaki pants whose word always held.

chapters.title: Where We Got Black Officials
chapters.summary: She explains the Connor v. Johnson case as two suits, one local and one state, over getting people elected to office and redrawing district lines, which produced many Black elected officials in Jackson and a Black congressman, and recalls voting every time for Percy Watson, who did not recognize her until about four years ago.

chapters.title: They Forget How They Got There
chapters.summary: Connor describes Percy Watson presenting her a framed proclamation in Jackson and inviting her to his Christmas party, her muted feeling about the honor, and her view that many Black elected officials do not want to be associated with her and forget how they reached office.

chapters.title: As Long As We Keep Bickering
chapters.summary: She questions whether Black elected officials have changed people's lives, names the main thing still missing as love among the people, and recalls being arrested in Jackson and put on the third floor away from the others while Charles Evers, who took over after Mr. Damon's death, ran out of his own demonstration.

chapters.title: Voting to Stop Demonstrations
chapters.summary: Connor recounts Monica, who first worked in Hattiesburg and stayed at her house before moving to Jackson, both of them jailed and bailed by her father on the same day, and a Jackson capitol protest against a special session voting to stop demonstrations.

chapters.title: Keep It Out of the News
chapters.topic: A Hometown Governor and Southern Today
chapters.summary: She explains that Governor Paul Johnson Jr. being from Hattiesburg led local white leaders to keep movement news quiet, including during school integration, and closes reflecting that the University of Southern Mississippi, once segregated, now has among the most Black students of any school in the state before the interview's thanks and sign-off.

loc_video.caption: Peggy Jean Connor oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, 2015 November 30
